Defendants/Cross Complainant Hua Qiao'S Application For Temporary Restraining Order And Preliminary Injunction (Per Ex Parte Order)
Off Calendar Mar-13-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Ron Lunski, Esq. of Runski & Stimpert LLP for Plaintiff Ying Gu.
Jake Freed, Esq. 415-276-6532 of Davis Wright Tremaine LLP for Defendant MIKI YANG, ACEH CAPITAL LLC, GREG WANG.
Joseph Breen, Esq. 415-602-4848 of Gordon & Rees for Defendant Christine Dang.
Vikram Subramanian, Esq. 408-436-0789 ext. 209 of SAC Attorneys LLP for Defendant Hua Qiao.

RULING - A R G U E D. Moving party Argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants/Cross Complainant Hua Qiao'S Application For Temporary Restraining Order And Preliminary Injunction.

Cross-Complainant Hua Qiao's application for a preliminary injunction freezing plaintiff Ying Gu's settlement proceeds is denied. Mr. Qiao does not present sufficient evidence showing that Ms. Gu has no other California assets. Thus Mr. Qiao fails to establish the required element of imminent irreparable harm. Moreover, the application lacks substantive merit. The cases that have allowed injunctions that freeze assets were in the context of a constructive trust and/or were issued to prevent the dissipation of wrongfully acquired assets. (Heckmann v. Ahmanson (1985) 168 Cal.Appp.3d 119, 134; Doyka v. Superior Court (1991) 233 Cal.App.3d 1134, 1137.) The authorities relied on by Mr. Qiao are not to the contrary. There are no facts showing that Ms. Gu defrauded Mr. Qiao out of the settlement proceeds and there is no basis to freeze her legally obtained assets. Per Doyka, Mr. Qiao is improperly seeking a prejudgment attachment without meeting the statutory requirements for such relief.

Counsel for Ms. Gu is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. (D302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-23-2017 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Ron U. Lunski of the law offices of Lunski & Stimpert LLP (415.426.3525) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, John Freed of the law offices of Davis Wright Tremaine LLP (415.276.4802) appears as counsel for defendant/demurring party

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTS CHRISTINE DANG, MIKI YANG, ACEH CAPITAL LLC and GREG WANG'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT is OVERRULED in its entirety. Reading the complaint liberally per CCP 452, plaintiffs allege sufficient facts to support the conversion claim against all of the defendants. (Amended Complaint, pars. 89-96.) The demurrer is overruled as to the fraud and negligent misrepresentation causes of action. Ms. Gu alleges that she was told on April 7, 2016 that she needed to tender the money prior to obtaining the sellers' signature. (Amended Complaint, pars. 13 and 33.) Hence, the fact that she tendered part of the money prior to signing the purchase agreement is irrelevant. It is also alleged that Ms. Gu tendered additional funds on April 11, 2017 after the alleged misrepresentations and that allegation supports reliance. Par. 33 of the amended complaint alleges that Mr. Yang told Ms. Gu to wire the money on April 7, 2016. The allegations in paragraph 34 against ACEH are actionable. The representation that ACEH represents the seller implies that ACEH will sell the property and convey title. The court overrules Ms. Dang's demurrer to the realtor claims (causes of action 3, 4, 6, 7, and 8). Pars. 8-9 allege that Ms. Dang was a sales manager for Ace Capital Group, that relationship was never disclosed, and Ms. Qiao (Ms. Gu's real estate agent) worked for Ms. Dang and Ms. Qiao was acting in the scope of her agency/employment. Ms. Qiao's alleged misconduct is imputed to Ms. Dang. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR No. 8189, email: ckaren@sftc.org. (D302)

MOTION TO DISMISS
Off Calendar May-16-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. RON LUNSKI OF LUNSKI & STIMPERT LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-426-3525 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. WOODY WU OF SAC ATTORNEYS, TEL. NO. 1-408-436-0789 EXT. 209 APPEARING FOR THE CROSS-COMPLAINANT HU QIAO.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Cross-defendant Ying Gu's motion to dismiss the cross-complaint filed by Hu Qiao on the grounds of inconvenient forum is denied. The supplemental evidence and briefing submitted by Ms. Gu is insufficient to satisfy her burden of showing that this court is a seriously inconvenient forum or that the public and private factors favor the resolution of the cross-complaint in China. There is a strong presumption in favor of Mr. Qiao's choice of forum because he is California resident. Contrary to Ms. Gu's assertion, all evidence and witnesses are not located exclusively in China. Mr. Qiao, the alleged victim, is undoubtedly a key witness. While Ms. Gu has shown that Chinese courts are generally not obligated to enforce subpoenas from foreign courts, she has not explained how she would be precluded from obtaining witnesses or evidence to defend against the cross-complaint should the action proceed in this court. In fact, the most likely scenario given the parties' apparent positions, is that the only liability witnesses that would be called on the cross-complaint are the parties themselves with Mr. Qiao testifying that Ms. Gu is responsible for his alleged injuries and Ms. Gu testifying that she isn't. While the parties provide no information as to damages witnesses and evidence, it is likely that, because Mr. Qiao resides in California, there are witnesses and evidence in California regarding his claimed injuries. Since Ms. Gu and Mr. Qiao are already litigating in this court, there is a strong presumption that judicial economy is best served by their litigating all of their claims against each other in this court, rather than litigating in two different fora. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR # 8189
